content:
Can AI Help Small Businesses Compete Beyond Their Size?
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Building Apostille-USA taught me that AI doesn’t just change workflows. It may be changing how expertise becomes visible.I’ve been thinking about something a lot over the past few weeks.
When my co-founder and I started Apostille-USA.com in 2023, we knew we were the new kids on the block.
There were companies in our industry that had been around for ten, fifteen, even twenty years. They had larger teams, established brands, years of customer reviews, and a big head start.
We had none of that.
Like most founders, we weren’t trying to disrupt an industry. We were just trying to build a company that solved a real problem and gave customers a great experience.
Fast forward three years, and something happened that genuinely surprised me.
We’ve been experimenting with a couple of AI visibility platforms to better understand how AI systems “see” our company. Recently, one of them ranked Apostille-USA #1 among more than 400 companies in our category for a group of high-intent customer questions.
I stared at that result for a while.
Not because I think one dashboard proves anything — it doesn’t.
But because it made me ask a much bigger question.
What if AI is quietly changing one of the oldest competitive advantages in business?
For decades, one of the biggest advantages established companies had was time.
Time to build a reputation.
Time to earn backlinks.
Time to accumulate reviews.
Time to become the company everyone recognized.
That made sense. Authority was something you earned over many years.
But AI seems to be introducing a different dynamic.
When someone asks ChatGPT or another AI assistant a question, it doesn’t simply ask, “Who’s been around the longest?”
It seems to ask something closer to:
“Who can best answer this question?”
That feels like an important shift.
Over the past year, we’ve spent an enormous amount of time improving the way we document what we know.
Not because we wanted to publish more content.
Because we wanted to answer customer questions better.
Instead of writing one generic page about apostilles, we started building detailed resources around the situations customers actually face every day.
FBI background checks.
Italian citizenship.
Power of attorney documents.
Corporate filings.
Embassy legalization.
Digital nomad visas.
Retirement abroad.
Federal versus state authentication.
These weren’t SEO exercises.
They were answers to questions we hear from real customers every single week.
Looking back, I don’t think we were simply creating content.
I think we were teaching AI how to understand our business.
People have started calling this GEO — Generative Engine Optimization — or AI SEO.
The terminology isn’t really what interests me.
The underlying idea is.
For years we optimized websites so search engines could find them.
Now we’re increasingly organizing knowledge so AI can understand it.
Those aren’t the same thing.
Search engines ranked pages.
AI is trying to understand organizations.
The more I think about it, the more I wonder if this could become a meaningful opportunity for small businesses.
Not because AI ignores experience.
It doesn’t.
Experience still matters enormously.
But AI can only learn from expertise it can actually see.
A company might have twenty years of incredible operational knowledge.
If most of that knowledge has never been documented publicly, AI has surprisingly little to work with.
Meanwhile, a three-year-old company that consistently publishes useful, well-organized knowledge may become visible much faster than people expect.
That doesn’t mean the newer company has more experience.
It means it has made its experience easier to understand.
I think that’s an important distinction.
One of the things I’ve enjoyed most about implementing AI over the past few years is that it keeps challenging assumptions I didn’t even realize I had.
I used to think authority was mostly about age.
Now I think clarity may be becoming just as important.
That’s exciting if you’re building a small company.
It means you don’t necessarily have to wait a decade before your expertise can become part of the conversation.
You still have to earn trust.
You still have to do great work.
You still have to solve real problems.
But if you consistently document what you know, AI may be able to help people discover that expertise much sooner than was possible before.
I don’t think AI replaces the advantages of established companies.
I do think it changes the speed at which smaller companies can become visible.
And if that’s true, I suspect we’re only beginning to understand what that means — not just for AI, but for entrepreneurship itself.
About the Author
Rugi Kavamahanga is the Founder & CEO of Apostille-USA.com, where he has spent the past several years implementing AI inside a compliance-sensitive legal document business. He writes about practical AI implementation, AI visibility, workflow design, and how small, founder-led companies can use AI to build stronger organizations.
